source.description: Backdated launch lesson raster sketch rebuilt around a small sprout with four leaves.
source.notes: The final should not be reused as the basis for a flower-bud lesson because the drawing problem is too similar.

process_strategy: Keep one leaning stem path fixed, then add the top bud, left leaf, right leaf, roots, and green pencil in the same small sprout silhouette.

final_elements.name: leaning stem
final_elements.description: The gently curved vertical stem path.

final_elements.name: soil arc
final_elements.description: The shallow ground line below the sprout.

final_elements.name: root marks
final_elements.description: The tiny curved roots under the stem.

final_elements.name: top bud
final_elements.description: The pointed bud shape at the top.

final_elements.name: left leaf
final_elements.description: The broad pointed leaf branching left.

final_elements.name: left leaf vein
final_elements.description: The central vein returning to the stem.

final_elements.name: right leaf
final_elements.description: The matching pointed leaf branching right.

final_elements.name: right leaf vein
final_elements.description: The central vein inside the right leaf.

frames.visible_job: Places the light leaning stem curve and shallow soil arc before any leaves are added.
Adds the pointed top bud centered over the stem while keeping the soil and stem guides visible.
Branches a broad pointed leaf to the left and connects its center vein back to the stem.

frames.step_name: Balance it with a right leaf
frames.visible_job: Adds the right-side leaf slightly higher so the sprout has the same balanced four-leaf silhouette as the finish.

final_step.visible_job: Adds tiny roots below the soil line and loose green pencil strokes along each leaf direction.

rejection_checks: The stem lean must stay consistent from the first frame through the finish.
The right leaf must not appear before its written step.
The final roots and green color must remain small finishing details.
Do not treat a flower bud or similar seedling as a meaningfully new daily subject.
